name: OC Housing Now
tagline: The state said build. We're making sure it happens.
mission:
California's state housing mandates — SB 9, SB 10, RHNA, Builder's Remedy — provide the legal framework for the housing OC needs. The problem isn't bad law. The problem is local governments actively evading compliance.

The City of Orange had its Housing Element REJECTED by the California Department of Housing and Community Development (HCD) in 2023. That rejection triggered Builder's Remedy — developers can now bypass local zoning for projects that include affordable units. Multiple applications are pending, including the Orange Heights 1,180-unit development. But the city is fighting every one of them.

87.5% of Orange County cities made their SB 9 ordinances MORE restrictive than state law requires. This is systematic noncompliance dressed up as local control.

This is not Austin's YIMBY fight. In Austin, you push local government to ADOPT pro-housing policy. In Orange County, you push local government to COMPLY WITH state housing mandates it's actively trying to evade. The legal tools already exist. What's missing is organized pressure to use them.

OC Housing Now exists because the enforcement gap between state mandate and local compliance is where housing dies. We track RHNA compliance across all 35 OC cities. We support Builder's Remedy applications. We show up at city councils when Housing Elements are on the agenda. We make the political cost of noncompliance higher than the cost of building.

The Irvine General Plan update proposes zoning for 57,656 new units — one of the largest zoning capacity increases in Southern California history. The Irvine Company (Donald Bren, America's richest real estate developer) controls ~75% of Irvine's apartment units. Whether the high-density scenario survives depends on organized pro-housing advocacy. We fight for maximum housing capacity because every unit not zoned is a family priced out.
theory_of_change: California's state housing mandates provide the legal framework. The bottleneck is local compliance. Organized advocates showing up at city councils, supporting Builder's Remedy applications, tracking RHNA compliance numbers across all 35 OC cities, and filing complaints with HCD when cities resist creates pressure for compliance. The legal leverage already exists — we activate it. In a purple county where council seats are decided by hundreds of votes, elected officials who block housing face electoral consequences when their noncompliance is made visible. We make it visible.

political_positioning.description: Pragmatic, broad-tent pro-housing coalition. Slightly right of center economically — we lead with property rights and market-based housing supply arguments, which resonate in OC's more conservative political environment. Socially moderate. The coalition includes YIMBY urbanists, property rights advocates, developers frustrated by local obstruction, renters squeezed by artificial scarcity, and state compliance advocates who believe the law should be followed. Similar to Austin YIMBY Action but shifted slightly right because OC's political center is further right than Austin's. Not affiliated with any party — we work with anyone who wants to build housing.

key_policy_positions: Enforce RHNA compliance across all 35 Orange County cities — track and publicize compliance status
Support Builder's Remedy applications in non-compliant cities including Orange
Oppose restrictive local SB 9 ordinances that undermine state housing law
Advocate for the high-density scenario in Irvine's General Plan update (57,656 units)
Push the City of Orange to adopt a compliant Housing Element and stop fighting state mandates
Support transit-oriented development along Metrolink corridors and the new OC Streetcar line
Eliminate or reduce parking minimums in OC cities to lower housing construction costs
File HCD complaints when cities adopt policies that obstruct state housing mandates
Support the Orange Heights 1,180-unit Builder's Remedy development
Advocate for ADU-friendly policies that go beyond minimum SB 9 requirements

active_campaigns.title: City of Orange Housing Element Accountability
active_campaigns.summary:
UPDATE (March 2026): The fight has shifted. The City of Orange appears to have achieved Housing Element compliance — it is NOT on Governor Newsom's March 25 noncompliant list (92% of CA communities now compliant). The city re-adopted its Housing Element in October 2023.

However, the Builder's Remedy legacy is where the real battle continues. Four Builder's Remedy applications filed during the noncompliance period total 696 units: Integral Communities (209 townhomes behind Village of Orange mall), Stonefield Development (138 apartments at Chapman-Yorba), and two Milan Capital projects (118 townhomes at Mara Brandman Horse Arena, 231 units at Sully-Miller quarry). The city has been fighting these — requiring zoning changes and general plan amendments despite Builder's Remedy, tactics struck down by LA County judges in similar cases.

AB 1893 (effective 2025) formalized Builder's Remedy as an entitlement pathway and prevents cities from requiring extra approvals — strengthening developers' legal position. Additionally, Californians for Homeownership sued the city alleging the approved Housing Element included parcels with deed restrictions forbidding housing.

The campaign shifts from 'force compliance' to 'ensure compliance produces actual housing.' Monitor whether the city processes the 696 pending Builder's Remedy units in good faith. The November 2026 council elections (3 seats + mayor) will determine whether Orange's housing posture improves or regresses.

⚡ MAY 21 UPDATE — HUNTINGTON BEACH FINED $160,000: On May 15, a court ordered Huntington Beach to pay $160K in penalties for 4.5+ years of housing noncompliance. Fines ESCALATE TO $50,000/MONTH starting June 2026 — compliance deadline: May 28 (5 DAYS). The US Supreme Court declined to hear HB's challenge (Feb 2026). AG Bonta: 'No city is above the law.' This is the most aggressive enforcement action against any OC city and a powerful precedent. The contrast is instructive: Yorba Linda complied proactively (Measure JJ, 90%+ voter support); HB fought and is now paying $50K/month. Newport Beach residents are pushing a ballot measure to reduce RHNA targets — another noncompliance fight ahead. Additionally, a Laguna Hills developer used Builder's Remedy for a 180-unit senior housing project ($130M) and has sued the OC Realtors trade group over alleged efforts to block it.

⚡ MAY 23 UPDATE: HUNTINGTON BEACH compliance deadline is May 28 — 5 DAYS. Fines escalate from $10K/month to $50K/month starting June 2026. Total accumulated fines: $160K. US Supreme Court declined HB's challenge (Feb 2026). AG Bonta: 'No city is above the law.' This is the most aggressive enforcement precedent in CA housing law — and it strengthens the case against any OC city contemplating noncompliance. Newport Beach ballot measure to reduce RHNA targets remains a threat. Governor's race (10 DAYS to June 2 primary) determines who enforces these mandates going forward.

⚡ MAY 24 UPDATE: 9 DAYS to June 2 primary. Huntington Beach compliance deadline May 28 — 4 DAYS. Fines escalate to $50K/month in June. Governor's race: three-way dead heat (Hilton 22%, Becerra 21%, Steyer 15% per EVITARUS poll May 14-16). The next governor controls housing enforcement — RHNA, Builder's Remedy, AG referrals.

⚡ MAY 25 UPDATE: 8 DAYS to June 2 primary. ⚡ Huntington Beach compliance deadline is May 28 — 3 DAYS. Court-ordered fines escalate to $50K/month in June if HB remains noncompliant. The court also required HB to adopt a compliant housing element by May 28. ⚡ OC Board of Supervisors voted unanimously (May 6) to deny an appeal against the 181-home Saddleback Meadows development on 222 acres — clearing a project that has faced opposition for over four decades. This sets a precedent for how the next BOS handles development conflicts. Trabuco Canyon is also seeing new housing despite wildfire risk concerns. The 166-unit affordable senior housing community 'The Orion' opened in the City of Orange ($63.6M public-private partnership). Governor's race three-way dead heat continues — the next governor determines enforcement posture.

⚡ MAY 26 UPDATE: 7 DAYS to June 2 primary. ⚡ Huntington Beach compliance deadline is May 28 — 2 DAYS. Court-ordered fines escalate to $50K/month in June if HB remains noncompliant. The enforcement framework is the backbone of every OC housing fight. Governor's race three-way dead heat continues (Hilton 22%, Becerra 21%, Steyer 15%). Saddleback Meadows (181 homes, approved May 6) and The Orion (166 affordable units, opened in City of Orange) show housing being delivered when political will exists.

**May 27 update:** 6 DAYS to June 2 primary. ⚡ HUNTINGTON BEACH: Court ordered $160K in penalties; city ordered to adopt compliant housing element by May 28 (TOMORROW) or face $50K/month fines starting June. Over 4.5 years behind schedule. ⚡ OC PIT COUNT: Homelessness down 13.7% to 6,321 — for the first time, more people sheltered (3,256) than unsheltered (3,065). Unsheltered down 27%. Housing supply is upstream of homelessness — this progress validates the enforcement framework. County budget pressures from Airport Fire payouts, sexual harassment settlements, and federal funding cuts add fiscal headwinds for housing investment. Governor's race dead heat continues — the next governor controls the enforcement posture that makes HB's fines possible.

⚡ MAY 28 UPDATE: HUNTINGTON BEACH COMPLIANCE DEADLINE IS TODAY. Court ordered the city to adopt a compliant housing element by May 28 or face $50K/month fines starting June (up from $10K/month since January 2025). Total penalties to date: $160K. All legal avenues exhausted — US Supreme Court declined to hear HB's challenge in February 2026. This is the most aggressive enforcement in CA housing law history. 5 DAYS until June 2 primary — the next Board of Supervisors will inherit this enforcement landscape. NEW PPIC POLL: Becerra 23%, Hilton 20% in governor's race — the next governor controls RHNA enforcement and Builder's Remedy.

**May 29 update:** ⚡ HUNTINGTON BEACH COMPLIANCE DEADLINE PASSED YESTERDAY (May 28). No confirmed report that the city adopted a compliant housing element by the deadline — if it missed the deadline, $50K/month fines begin in June (up from $10K/month since January 2025). Total penalties to date: $160K. One HCD source indicates HB may have received state approval for its housing plan, but confirmation is pending. ⚡ GARDEN GROVE CHEMICAL CRISIS: A GKN Aerospace tank containing 7,000 gallons of methyl methacrylate forced the evacuation of 50,000 residents (May 21-26). Governor Newsom declared a state of emergency. BOS ratified the federal emergency declaration. Class-action lawsuit filed. DA launched criminal probe. Garden Grove and Stanton residents demanding accountability and financial reparations. This crisis dominates the final-week campaign conversation and tests emergency management capacity. ⚡ 4 DAYS to June 2 primary. PPIC poll (May 14-18): Becerra 23%, Hilton 20%, Steyer 15%, Bianco 13%, Porter 12%. A Becerra-Hilton top-two is the most likely outcome — the next governor controls RHNA enforcement and Builder's Remedy.

**May 30 update:** ⚡ 3 DAYS to June 2 primary. Final weekend — in-person early voting opens at some locations. NEW UC BERKELEY IGS POLL (May 19-24): Becerra 25%, Hilton 21%, Steyer 19%. Becerra's surge confirmed — up from 5% in March IGS poll. The race for second is TIGHTENING: Steyer (19%) is now within 2 points of Hilton (21%), making a two-Democrat top-two (Becerra + Steyer) plausible for the first time. This would be favorable for housing enforcement — both support strong RHNA enforcement. ⚡ Huntington Beach's May 28 compliance deadline has passed — no public confirmation of adoption. If noncompliant, $50K/month fines begin June. ⚡ Garden Grove crisis aftermath: Governor Newsom initiated safety record review of GKN and similar chemical plants. Multiple law firms offering free consultations. The crisis has permanently embedded industrial safety in the D4 race. The next supervisor inherits crisis response costs alongside the $10.8B budget.

⚡ MAY 31 UPDATE: 2 DAYS to June 2 primary. ALL VOTE CENTERS OPEN TODAY (10am-6pm). ⚡ HUNTINGTON BEACH COMPLIANCE CONFIRMED: California HCD indicates Huntington Beach has received state approval for its housing plan, bringing the city back into compliance after falling out in 2015. The compliance was achieved under sustained pressure from Governor Newsom, AG Bonta, and escalating court-ordered fines ($160K + $50K/month starting June). The plan allows opportunity for at least 413 new affordable homes. This is a major enforcement success — demonstrating that the state pressure model works even against the most resistant jurisdictions. The question shifts to implementation: will HB approve projects in good faith? ⚡ FINAL EMERSON POLL (May 27-28): Becerra 28%, Steyer 22%, Hilton 21%. Steyer has PASSED Hilton — a two-Democrat top-two (Becerra + Steyer) is now the most likely outcome, which would be favorable for housing enforcement. YIMBY Action endorsed Steyer. Both support strong RHNA enforcement.

**June 1 update:** Orange remains NOT on noncompliant list. Huntington Beach achieved compliance after $160K fines and May 28 deadline — adopted zoning for 413+ affordable homes. U.S. Supreme Court declined HB’s challenge (February 2026). AB 1893 strengthens Builder’s Remedy. Monitor Orange’s four BR applications (696 units). CA PRIMARY TOMORROW.

**June 3 update:** ⚡ JUNE 2 PRIMARY RESULTS: Tim Shaw (R, 17,895) and Connor Traut (D, 17,827) advance to November in an ultra-tight D4 race — just 68 votes separating them. Fred Jung and Rose Espinoza eliminated. The NAR’s $125K investment in Shaw proved decisive. The November Shaw vs. Traut contest determines whether D4 maintains the Democratic lean that supports housing enforcement. In D5, incumbent Katrina Foley (D, 47,953) leads Diane Dixon (R, 45,368) — if Foley holds, the 3-2 Democratic BOS majority survives.

⚡ GOVERNOR: Hilton (R) 27%, Becerra (D) 26% with ~50% counted — both likely advance. A Hilton governorship could weaken RHNA enforcement, Builder’s Remedy implementation, and AG referrals that are the backbone of every OC housing fight. Becerra as AG was instrumental in the HB enforcement.

⚡ HUNTINGTON BEACH COMPLIANCE CONFIRMED: HCD approved HB’s housing plan, ending 4.5+ years of noncompliance under sustained state pressure ($160K in fines, $50K/month escalation). The plan zones for 413+ affordable homes. This is a major enforcement success proving the state pressure model works.

21.7% turnout countywide, 414,303 ballots counted. Canvass period through July 3.

**June 4 update:** UPDATED PRIMARY RESULTS: D4 — Shaw (R) 18,794 vs Traut (D) 18,304 (490-vote gap). D5 — Foley (D) 48,745 vs Dixon (R) 46,256. US Supreme Court declined Huntington Beach’s housing law challenge. Court-ordered $50K/month fines begin June if HB noncompliant by May 28 deadline.

**June 5 update:** ⚡ HUNTINGTON BEACH DELAYED AGAIN: City Council voted 6-0 on June 3 to CONTINUE the housing element update vote to the June 16 meeting (Councilmember Van Der Mark absent). $50K/month fines have STARTED as of June 2026 — accumulating monthly until the city cures its violations. The 6-0 continuation vote (not rejection) signals likely passage on June 16, but every month of delay adds $50K in penalties on top of the $160K already ordered. ⚡ CA GOVERNOR PRIMARY (57% counted): Hilton (R) 27%, Becerra (D) 26%, Steyer 20%. Katie Porter conceded. Top two advance to November — certification by July 3. A Hilton governorship could weaken RHNA enforcement. ⚡ D4: Shaw (R) leads Traut (D) by 490 votes (18,794 vs 18,304). D5: Foley (D) leads Dixon (R) 48,745 vs 46,256. Canvass through July 10.

**June 7 update:** ⚡ HUNTINGTON BEACH delayed housing element adoption to June 16 — 9 DAYS. $50K/month fines accruing. ⚡ D5 ALARM: Dixon (R) has taken the lead over Foley (D) — 62,640 to 58,654. Combined with Shaw (R) leading Traut (D) by ~1,600 in D4, the Democratic BOS majority that supports housing enforcement faces double jeopardy. Governor: Becerra (D) takes slight lead over Hilton (R). OC Streetcar safety testing started June 3 — March 2027 launch confirmed. The enforcement framework that makes local housing advocacy effective depends on who controls the BOS and the governor's mansion in November.

**June 8 update:** Huntington Beach housing element vote is June 16 — 8 DAYS. Council voted 6-0 to postpone (Van Der Mark absent). $50K/month fines accruing. The housing plan zones for ~13,000 new units and allows 413+ affordable homes. BOS canvass update expected today — D4 Shaw (R) leads Traut (D) by ~1,621; D5 Dixon (R) leads Foley (D) by ~4,000. TOMORROW (June 9) is the last day for mail ballot receipt. If both Republicans win November, the enforcement framework that supports local housing advocacy is at serious risk.

**June 9 update:** Huntington Beach housing element vote is June 16 — 7 DAYS. $50K/month fines accruing — over 4.5 years noncompliant. All legal avenues exhausted. TODAY is the last day for mail ballot receipt. Governor: Becerra (D) has overtaken Hilton (R) 26.7% to 26.4% — a Democratic governor protects the RHNA/Builder's Remedy enforcement framework. D4 Shaw (R) leads Traut (D) by ~1,600+; D5 Dixon (R) leads Foley (D). If both Republicans win November, housing enforcement support at the county level is at serious risk.

**June 10 update:** Huntington Beach housing element vote June 16 — 6 DAYS. $50K/month fines NOW ACCRUING as of June — $160K retroactive already owed. Court ordered Builder's Remedy project approvals within 60-90 days. First successful Builder's Remedy court case won in La Cañada Flintridge (80 units) — OC cities on notice. Mail ballot deadline passed yesterday. Governor: Becerra (D) leads Hilton (R) 26.7% to 26.4%. D4: Shaw (R) leads by ~1,600; D5: Dixon (R) leads by ~4,000. If both Republicans win November, county-level housing enforcement support seriously at risk. OC Streetcar launch pushed to March 2027.

**June 11 update:** Huntington Beach housing vote June 16 — 5 DAYS. Council previously postponed 6-0 (Van Der Mark absent). $50K/month fines NOW ACCRUING — $160K+ retroactive already owed. Court ordered Builder's Remedy project approvals within 60-90 days. ⚡ FBI/EPA raided GKN Aerospace yesterday — federal criminal probe escalates Garden Grove accountability, demonstrating consequences of inadequate corporate/government oversight. D4: Shaw (R) leads by ~1,600; D5: Dixon (R) leads by ~4,000 — both trending Republican. OC Streetcar remains at March 2027 launch.

**June 12 update:** Huntington Beach housing vote June 16 — 4 DAYS. City is now PAST the court-ordered May 28 deadline — $50K/month fines actively accruing. Total owed: $160K+. Court ordered Builder's Remedy project approvals within 60-90 days. ⚡ Democrats pulled ahead in BOTH D4 and D5 supervisor races — if leads hold through certification, the 3-2 Democratic majority is preserved going into November, maintaining county-level housing enforcement support. Governor: Becerra (D) extending lead over Hilton (R) at 88% counted — favorable for state housing enforcement posture. GKN committed $5M total in community support but chemical extraction still delayed.

**June 13 update:** Huntington Beach housing vote June 16 — 3 DAYS. City posted revised Draft Housing Element Update on June 8. Council voted 6-0 on June 2 to postpone to June 16. $50K/month fines actively accruing since June 1 — total owed now $210K+. Court ordered Builder's Remedy project approvals within 60-90 days. US Supreme Court declined to hear HB's challenge in February — all legal avenues exhausted. D5 supervisor race seesawing — Dixon (R) appears to have retaken lead from Foley (D); county-level housing enforcement posture at stake. Governor: Becerra (D) extending lead, favorable for state housing enforcement.

**June 14 update:** Huntington Beach housing vote June 16 — 2 DAYS. Revised Draft Housing Element posted June 8 — zones for ~13,000 new units. $50K/month fines actively accruing since June 1 — total owed now $260K+. Court ordered Builder's Remedy project approvals within 60-90 days. All legal avenues exhausted. D5: Dixon (R) consolidated lead over Foley (D) — county-level housing enforcement posture at stake. Governor: Becerra (D) extending lead, favorable for state housing enforcement.

**June 15 update:** ⚡ Huntington Beach housing vote TOMORROW (June 16). Revised Draft Housing Element posted June 8 — zones for ~13,000 new units. $50K/month fines actively accruing since June 1; total owed ~$310K+. US Supreme Court declined to hear in February — all legal avenues exhausted. Court ordered Builder's Remedy project approvals within 60-90 days. Governor: Becerra (D) extending lead, favorable for state housing enforcement.

**June 16 update:** ⚡ Huntington Beach housing element vote is TODAY. Council voted 6-0 on June 3 (Van Der Mark absent) to continue the item to this meeting. Revised Draft Housing Element posted June 8 — zones for ~13,000 new units. $50K/month fines actively accruing since June 1; total owed ~$335K+. US Supreme Court declined in February — all legal avenues exhausted. Court ordered Builder's Remedy project approvals within 60-90 days. Governor race: Becerra (D) ~26.7% vs Hilton (R) ~26.4% — extremely tight.

**June 17 update:** Huntington Beach council met yesterday (June 16) for the housing element vote — result not yet confirmed. $50K/month fines continue; total owed ~$385K+. Could escalate to $100K/month in July, and up to $900K/month with receivership risk. HB is 4.5+ years behind schedule and the only noncompliant city in OC. First general election poll: Becerra (D) 52% vs Hilton (R) 31% — a Becerra win maintains aggressive state housing enforcement. D5: Foley (D) reclaims narrow lead over Dixon (R), 47.0% vs 46.8%.

**June 18 update:** ⚡ MAJOR VICTORY — Huntington Beach City Council voted 6-1 on June 16 to adopt the housing element after years of legal resistance. Chad Williams sole dissent. City now zones for 13,000+ new residences — was the ONLY noncompliant city in all of Orange County, 4.5+ years behind schedule. Mayor McKeon cited mounting fines (~$100K+ accrued) but signaled the fight may continue at the ballot box. Judge to rule next month on increasing fines from $50K to $150K/month — adoption may reduce or eliminate future penalties. Several councilmembers described the vote as being "beaten into submission." Becerra (D) +21 over Hilton (R) in first general poll — Becerra win maintains aggressive state enforcement. D5: Foley (D) 47.0% vs Dixon (R) 46.8% — registrar count TODAY.

**June 19 update:** HB housing element adopted June 16 but CA HCD must still review and certify the plan — more back-and-forth likely. Several HB councilmembers signaled continued resistance via ballot measures. Judge ruling next month on increasing fines to $150K/month. ⚡ D5: Dixon (R) takes lead over Foley (D) ~48.96% to ~45.08% — if Dixon wins in November, could shift Board posture on state housing mandates.

**June 20 update:** HB housing element adopted June 16 (6-1) — HCD review and certification pending. Several HB councilmembers signaled continued resistance via ballot measures despite mounting fines (~$100K+ accrued). Judge ruling next month on increasing fines to $150K/month. Becerra (D) +21 over Hilton (R) in first general poll — Becerra win would maintain aggressive state housing enforcement. Grand Jury response deadline June 30 (10 DAYS).
active_campaigns.theory_of_change: Compliance without implementation is performative. The city achieved technical compliance, but if it continues to obstruct the 696 Builder's Remedy units, the compliance is hollow. AB 1893 gives developers stronger legal tools, but legal rights without organized local support lead to protracted fights. Our role shifts from forcing compliance to ensuring the compliance produces actual housing. Public tracking of Builder's Remedy application processing, attendance at council hearings, and coordination with California YIMBY on enforcement keep pressure on the city to act in good faith.
next_actions: Track status of all four pending Builder's Remedy applications (696 total units) through the approval process
Monitor whether the city complies with AB 1893 by processing applications without requiring extra zoning approvals
Attend every council meeting where Builder's Remedy projects are on the agenda
Track Californians for Homeownership lawsuit progress — their challenge strengthens the enforcement framework
Coordinate with California YIMBY on AB 1893 enforcement and state-level compliance pressure
Prepare for November 2026 council elections — evaluate candidates on housing and Builder's Remedy positions
Publicize Newsom's March 25 enforcement escalation (15 noncompliant cities, AG referrals) as context for Orange's obligations
Prepare for Shaw vs. Traut D4 general election — evaluate housing positions for November
Monitor Huntington Beach Housing Element implementation — compliance is only step one
Track CA governor race count (canvass through July 3) — enforcement depends on who wins

bridge_building.opposition_steelman: Reasonable skeptics of City of Orange Housing Element Accountability often worry about unintended costs, fairness across neighborhoods, and whether promised benefits will be measured transparently rather than assumed.

cross_partisan_appeals.framing: Define clear outcomes, timelines, and guardrails before scaling spending or mandates.
cross_partisan_appeals.evidence: Campaigns with published milestones and public reporting earn broader support and allow mid-course corrections.
cross_partisan_appeals.example_dialogue: "If City of Orange Housing Element Accountability is worth doing, it is worth measuring with transparent benchmarks so everyone can see what is working."

cross_partisan_appeals.framing: Implement change in ways that reduce disruption and share benefits across existing residents and newcomers.
cross_partisan_appeals.evidence: Phased implementation and regular public feedback often lower backlash while improving policy durability.
cross_partisan_appeals.example_dialogue: "We can move City of Orange Housing Element Accountability forward while still listening to neighborhood-level concerns and adjusting implementation details."

common_ground: Public policy should solve real problems, not just signal values.
Residents deserve clear communication about tradeoffs.
Programs should be adjusted when evidence shows gaps.
conversation_traps_to_avoid: Assuming all critics are acting in bad faith.
Overpromising speed or certainty on complex implementation.
Using national partisan frames for local decisions.
when_to_acknowledge_uncertainty: When baseline data is incomplete or lagged.
When legal or budget decisions are still pending.
When implementation depends on partner agencies outside our control.
honest_limits: This campaign cannot alone fix every upstream structural problem.
We cannot guarantee identical outcomes in every neighborhood.
We rely on sustained public participation and institutional follow-through.

active_campaigns.title: Irvine General Plan — Implementation & Irvine Company Monitoring
active_campaigns.summary:
UPDATE (May 7, 2026): Irvine City Council APPROVED the zoning text amendment on April 14, enabling the 'Alternative Open Space Approach' for Planning Area 12 (Oak Creek). Broader project approvals for the 3,100-unit development remain pending. Mayor Larry Agran has signaled he will ensure residents can vote on the project via a future ballot measure.

⚡ ELECTION UPDATE: Ballots are in voters' hands — 1.9M mailed starting May 2. Mail-in voting open. ⚡ VOTER REGISTRATION DEADLINE PASSED (May 18). 13 DAYS until June 2 primary. The CNN governor's debate (May 5) was fiercely combative — Becerra took heavy hits, no clear winner, Democratic fragmentation persists. The next governor controls RHNA enforcement posture.

⚡ BOARD OF SUPERVISORS — UPDATED FINANCE DATA: District 5 is attracting the most outside money — Foley's Deputy Sheriffs union spending $213K, Lincoln Club spending $66K against her. Dixon edges Foley in cash ($232K vs $225K). In District 4, National Association of Realtors spending $125K+ supporting Tim Shaw.

The Irvine Company's revised package includes: $96 million in community benefit fees, 2,000 rent vouchers ($72 million value), land donations, a 50-acre nature park, and a new elementary school within the 3,100-unit residential village.

Opposition remains active: Former mayors Sheridan and Shea gathering signatures (~19,000 needed) for a citizen's initiative.

TRANSIT CONNECTION: OC Streetcar ($649M, 4.15 miles, Santa Ana to Garden Grove) launch target AUGUST 2026. In active daytime street testing.

The General Plan (ADOPTED August 13, 2024) with 57,656 units is in implementation phase. November 2026 council elections (3 seats + mayor) remain critical.

⚡ MAY 28 UPDATE: OC Streetcar now 92-95% complete with eight Siemens S700 vehicles delivered. Active street testing on Santa Ana Boulevard. Launch target AUGUST 2026. 5 DAYS until June 2 primary — November council elections (3 seats + mayor) critical for General Plan implementation.

**May 29 update:** ⚡ OC Streetcar now 92-95% complete with active street testing on Santa Ana Boulevard. Eight Siemens S700 vehicles delivered. Launch target remains AUGUST 2026. The $649M project links the Santa Ana Regional Transportation Center to the Harbor Transit Center in Garden Grove — but the Garden Grove chemical crisis (50K evacuated May 21-26) temporarily disrupted the area. 4 DAYS to June 2 primary — November council elections (3 seats + mayor) critical for General Plan implementation.

**May 31 update:** 2 DAYS to June 2 primary. All vote centers open today. The OC Streetcar remains on track for AUGUST 2026 launch despite the Garden Grove crisis disruption. The Irvine Company's 3,100-unit Oak Creek proposal and the 2,500-unit UC Irvine-area conversion represent 5,600+ new units advancing. November 2026 council elections (3 seats + mayor) remain critical for General Plan implementation. Former mayors' signature-gathering initiative (~19,000 needed) creates ballot measure risk. FINAL EMERSON POLL: Becerra 28%, Steyer 22%, Hilton 21% — a pro-housing governor strengthens the RHNA framework that underpins Irvine's ambitious zoning.

**June 1 update:** Planning Commission voted 7-0 for Oak Creek zoning amendment. Irvine Company revised: 50+ acre nature park + 3,100 homes + 2,500 near UC Irvine. City Council to hear item. Agran signaling possible ballot measure but nothing scheduled. November elections (3 seats + mayor) critical. CA PRIMARY TOMORROW: Governor race matters for enforcement posture.

**June 4 update:** Oak Creek zoning amendment approved April 14. 50+ acre nature park, under 3,000 units. Developer seeking final approval summer 2026, construction early 2027. OC Streetcar revenue service DELAYED to March 2027.

**June 5 update:** ⚡ OC STREETCAR DELAY CONFIRMED: OCTA staff confirmed revenue service pushed from August 2026 to March 2027 — the project has slipped from its original 2021 target through multiple delays. Eight Siemens S700 vehicles delivered, active street testing continues on Santa Ana Boulevard. The delay affects transit-oriented development timelines along the corridor. Oak Creek: Irvine Company seeking final council approval summer 2026 for under 3,000 units with 50+ acre nature park, construction expected early 2027. Former mayors' signature-gathering initiative (~19,000 needed) creates ballot measure risk. CA governor primary (57% counted): Hilton (R) 27%, Becerra (D) 26%.

**June 7 update:** Irvine Company's Oak Creek development revised to include a 50-acre public nature park at the heart of the development (approved 7-0 by Planning Commission in March). Environmental and traffic reviews underway. Formal public hearings expected late 2026 with a potential public vote due to 1988 open space designation. The project is far from a done deal — council approval, environmental review, and possible ballot measure all remain.

**June 19 update:** ⚡ D5: Dixon (R) takes lead over Foley (D) in latest count — supervisor race affects countywide housing policy coordination. Irvine General Plan implementation continuing; Oak Creek Golf Course conversion council vote still pending.

**June 20 update:** Oak Creek Golf Course conversion: environmental and traffic reviews underway; formal public hearings expected late 2026. Irvine Company's revised plan includes 50-acre nature park + ~3,000 housing units. Former mayors gathering signatures for citizen's initiative challenging the 1988 open space designation. November council elections (3 seats + mayor) remain critical for implementation. D5 supervisor race outcome affects countywide housing coordination.
active_campaigns.theory_of_change: The General Plan adoption was the policy win. Now the risk is implementation failure — the plan zones for 57,656 units but private developers must actually build them. The Irvine Company's 3,100-unit Oak Creek proposal is the bellwether: if it proceeds smoothly, it signals the plan is translating into housing. If it stalls, the plan is paper. Supporting pro-housing council candidates in November 2026 protects the implementation trajectory. Monitoring the Irvine Company's pricing and project mix ensures the monopoly holder doesn't capture all the value while constraining supply.
next_actions: Track Irvine Company Oak Creek Golf Course conversion hearings and approval timeline
Monitor implementation of General Plan zoning changes — which areas are being rezoned first

active_campaigns.title: RHNA Compliance Tracking Across OC
active_campaigns.summary:
UPDATE (May 8, 2026): Voice of OC reports that many OC cities are failing at low-income housing despite RHNA compliance on paper. The gap between zoned capacity and actual construction is widest in the very low and low-income categories.

⚡ MAY 6 — SADDLEBACK MEADOWS APPROVED: Board of Supervisors voted UNANIMOUSLY to deny appeal challenging 181-home development on 222 acres near Live Oak Canyon/El Toro Rd. Project was delayed 40+ years by legal challenges and opposition. The unanimous vote — including Republican supervisors — signals the current Board's pro-development posture.

⚡ NEW FUNDING: OCHCD released 2026 Supportive Housing NOFA — $20.9M for acquisition, construction, and rehabilitation of supportive housing, plus up to 100 Project Based Vouchers for extremely low-income homeless households. Combined with $35.1M HHAP state funding and the proposed $10B state Affordable Housing Bond, this is the largest injection of housing funding in recent OC history.

⚡ ELECTION CONNECTION: The CNN governor's debate (May 5) saw housing affordability as the #2 voter issue (20%). The next governor controls RHNA enforcement, Builder's Remedy, and AG referrals. Ballots in voters' hands — 13 DAYS to primary. ⚡ VOTER REGISTRATION DEADLINE PASSED (May 18). LAist published a comprehensive voter guide on the District 4 Board of Supervisors race. The five-way Democratic fragmentation raises the risk of two Republicans making the top-two, which could weaken housing enforcement statewide.

⚡ SPECIFIC CITY SHORTFALLS: Anaheim has permitted only 3,048 of its required 17,453 units — over 3,000 low-income units short. Fullerton: 1,384 of 13,209 units (11,825 remaining). Huntington Beach, Costa Mesa, Newport Beach, Garden Grove, Tustin, Westminster, and Fountain Valley all behind schedule.

⚡ AFFORDABLE HOUSING MONTH: OC's 2026 Affordable Housing Awards ceremony May 14 — 121,434 low-income renter households in OC still lack access to an affordable home.

Newport Beach voters will decide in November 2026 whether to replace the state-approved housing plan with the 'Responsible Housing Initiative' allowing far fewer new units.

**May 18 update:** Voice of OC analysis confirms the affordability gap: over 25,000 units built in OC (2021-2024) but only 17% (4,361) qualify as affordable. Santa Ana and Anaheim produced 47% of OC's low-income housing. Aliso Viejo and Huntington Beach produced zero affordable units. OCHCD released a $20.9M Supportive Housing NOFA for federal HOME funds. Additionally, $35.1M in HHAP funding from the state. Combined with the Orange County Housing Authority's new PBV program for homeless individuals (applications opened April 6), significant funding is now flowing — the question is whether it reaches the cities with the greatest need and greatest resistance.

⚡ AB 1907: The Board of Supervisors unanimously backed AB 1907, which would exempt affordable housing, supportive housing, and emergency shelters from CEQA — a major potential accelerant for affordable development in OC.

⚡ VOTER REGISTRATION DEADLINE PASSED (May 18). Ballots are in voters' hands for the June 2 primary. 13 DAYS until primary.

**May 27 update:** 6 DAYS to June 2 primary. OC PIT Count shows homelessness down 13.7% to 6,321 — for the first time, more sheltered (3,256) than unsheltered (3,065). This validates the dual approach: enforcement + housing supply. But less than 1 in 5 shelter residents exit to permanent housing — the RHNA compliance gap is the bottleneck. Huntington Beach compliance deadline is May 28 (TOMORROW) — $50K/month fines start June. Aliso Viejo and HB still at zero affordable units produced. County budget pressures from Airport Fire payouts and federal funding cuts threaten housing investment.

Build and maintain a public dashboard tracking all 35 Orange County cities' RHNA compliance. Most OC cities are behind, especially for very low and low-income units. Making this data publicly accessible creates accountability pressure.

**May 31 update:** 2 DAYS to June 2 primary. ⚡ HUNTINGTON BEACH COMPLIANCE CONFIRMED per California HCD — the city's housing plan received state approval, ending the 4.5+ year noncompliance saga. Accumulated penalties: $160K+. The compliance validates the state pressure model and removes one of the most prominent enforcement targets. However, the question shifts to implementation — will HB approve housing projects in good faith? Meanwhile, RHNA shortfalls persist across OC: Anaheim (3,048/17,453), Fullerton (1,384/13,209), and multiple other cities behind schedule. Only 17% of 25,000+ units built in OC (2021-2024) qualify as affordable. The June 2 primary determines who inherits the $10.8B budget and the enforcement landscape.

**June 1 update:** ⚡ HUNTINGTON BEACH COMPLIANCE ACHIEVED. 92% of CA compliant (480/525). OC: 25,000 units built 2021-2024, only 17% affordable. RHNA alarm: Anaheim 3,048/17,453; Fullerton 1,384/13,209. $20.9M NOFA released. CA PRIMARY TOMORROW: Governor race determines RHNA enforcement posture.

**June 4 update:** MBTA Communities Act 5-year report (January 2026) shows 7,000 new units in pipeline — comparison model for CA RHNA enforcement. Huntington Beach fines escalating. Certification of primary results by July 10.

**June 5 update:** ⚡ HUNTINGTON BEACH STILL NONCOMPLIANT: Council voted 6-0 on June 3 to delay housing element vote to June 16. $50K/month fines accumulating since June. The 6-0 continuation (not rejection) suggests compliance is likely on June 16 — but the delay costs continue. CA governor primary (57% counted): Hilton (R) 27%, Becerra (D) 26%. Katie Porter conceded. Certification by July 3. RHNA enforcement posture depends on who wins. GKN Aerospace donated $3M to United Way OC + pledged $1M — cleanup delayed as specialized transport trucks haven't arrived. 44 lawsuits filed.
active_campaigns.theory_of_change: What gets measured gets managed. A public, regularly updated compliance dashboard turns abstract state mandates into concrete, city-by-city accountability. Media can use it for stories. Advocates can prioritize cities with the worst compliance. Residents can see how their city compares. This is the infrastructure that makes all other housing advocacy more effective.
next_actions: Compile RHNA allocation data for all 35 OC cities from SCAG (Southern California Association of Governments)
Research each city's Housing Element status and permitted unit counts
Design dashboard format — city-by-city comparison with compliance percentage by income category
Identify data sources for ongoing tracking (HCD reports, city annual progress reports)
Partner with Kennedy Commission on data sharing and methodology
Launch beta dashboard and solicit feedback from partner organizations

active_campaigns.title: Builder's Remedy Awareness & Support
active_campaigns.summary:
Many OC residents, developers, and even city officials don't fully understand Builder's Remedy — the state law provision that allows projects with affordable units to bypass local zoning in cities with non-compliant Housing Elements. We educate residents about what it means, support developers who file Builder's Remedy applications, and counter misinformation from NIMBYs who frame it as a threat rather than a consequence of noncompliance.

**June 1 update:** HB compliance removes one prominent noncompliant city from BR exposure. AB 1893 strengthens developers’ position. Orange has four pending BR applications (696 units). Saddleback Meadows (181 homes, unanimous BOS May 6) signals D majority supports housing. CA PRIMARY TOMORROW.

**June 4 update:** US Supreme Court declined HB’s challenge — major precedent strengthening state housing mandates. SB 1037 (Weiner) $10K-$50K/month escalating penalties now being enforced.

**June 5 update:** HB council delayed housing element vote to June 16 — but 6-0 continuation vote (not rejection) suggests compliance is likely. Orange's four pending BR applications (696 units) remain in pipeline. CA governor primary: Hilton (R) 27%, Becerra (D) 26% with 57% counted — BR enforcement depends on the governor. Certification by July 3.
active_campaigns.theory_of_change: Builder's Remedy is powerful but underutilized because developers face political risk and community opposition. Organized community support for Builder's Remedy projects changes the political dynamic — developers know they're not alone, councils know there's constituency for the projects, and residents understand that the 'threat' is actually a consequence of their city's own noncompliance. Education reduces opposition; organized support enables development.
next_actions: Create explainer content: What is Builder's Remedy? Why does it apply in your city?
Map all OC cities currently in Builder's Remedy status
Document pending Builder's Remedy applications across OC
Develop template public comment supporting Builder's Remedy projects
Coordinate with developers filing Builder's Remedy applications to provide community support
Counter misinformation campaigns by city officials who frame Builder's Remedy as an attack on local control

aligned_organizations.name: People for Housing OC
aligned_organizations.relationship: Core local ally — strongest YIMBY voice in OC with only 2 staffers. We amplify their work and provide volunteer capacity.

aligned_organizations.name: Kennedy Commission
aligned_organizations.relationship: Affordable housing research partner. Their data on housing need and income categories strengthens our RHNA compliance advocacy.

aligned_organizations.name: California YIMBY
aligned_organizations.relationship: State-level ally providing legal expertise on housing mandates, Builder's Remedy, and RHNA compliance. They connect us to the statewide movement.

aligned_organizations.name: Irvine Community Land Trust
aligned_organizations.relationship: Aligned on permanently affordable housing goals. Their community land trust model provides an alternative development path.

aligned_organizations.name: Austin YIMBY Action
aligned_organizations.relationship: Cross-location ally. Same pro-housing mission in a different political context. Austin pushes for policy adoption; we push for policy compliance. Share strategy and lessons.

aligned_organizations.name: OC Abundance Project
aligned_organizations.relationship: Cross-movement ally. Every housing unit built is a family formation enabler. Our housing supply advocacy strengthens their family-sized housing campaign.

engagement_pipeline.awareness: Social media, Voice of OC coverage, RHNA compliance dashboard as shareable content. Explainer content on Builder's Remedy and state housing mandates.
engagement_pipeline.interest: Attend a Housing Element public hearing or read our city-by-city compliance tracker. Understand how your city compares on RHNA targets.
engagement_pipeline.action: Submit public comment supporting housing at your city council. Show up at a Builder's Remedy project hearing. Contact your council member about Housing Element compliance. Share compliance data on social media.
engagement_pipeline.leadership: Join the RHNA tracking research team. Lead advocacy at a specific city council. Coordinate with California YIMBY on state-level compliance pressure. Recruit volunteers for hearing attendance.

immediate.action: VOTE in June 2 primary — 2 DAYS. All vote centers open today (10am-6pm) and tomorrow. Election Day June 2 (7am-8pm). D4 and D5 races determine board composition and housing enforcement posture
Track Huntington Beach housing plan implementation — HCD confirmed compliance, but monitor whether city approves projects in good faith or continues resistance at project-approval level
Monitor June 2 governor's race results — FINAL EMERSON POLL shows Becerra 28%, Steyer 22%, Hilton 21%. A Becerra-Steyer top-two would be favorable for RHNA enforcement
Continue tracking all four pending Builder's Remedy applications in Orange (696 total units) — AB 1893 strengthens legal position. Monitor council hearings

medium_term.action: Launch RHNA compliance tracker v1 for all 35 OC cities — include the specific shortfalls: Anaheim (3,048/17,453), Fullerton (1,384/13,209)
Organize hearing attendance for Orange Builder's Remedy projects — track all four applications (696 total units)
Track Irvine General Plan implementation and Oak Creek Golf Course conversion — Planning Commission recommended approval unanimously, Council vote pending
Track OCHCD $20.9M NOFA awards — which projects receive funding, which neighborhoods gain supportive housing capacity

strategic.action: Build multi-org coalition for RHNA enforcement across OC — People for Housing OC, Kennedy Commission, California YIMBY, labor groups
strategic.notes: Combined advocacy amplifies pressure on non-compliant cities. Coalition letters carry more weight than individual org voices.

strategic.action: Evaluate November 2026 City of Orange council candidates on housing positions
strategic.notes: 3 council seats + mayor on ballot. Coordinate with OC Purple Accountability on candidate questionnaires and scorecards.

strategic.action: File HCD complaints against the most egregiously non-compliant OC cities
strategic.notes: Legal pressure backstops advocacy pressure. Document noncompliance patterns and coordinate with California YIMBY on complaint strategy.

bridge_building.opposition_steelman: Residents worry that aggressive housing targets can overload roads, schools, and utilities, and they question whether new approvals will deliver truly affordable homes instead of market-rate units beyond reach for local workers.

cross_partisan_appeals.framing: Plan growth with enforceable infrastructure sequencing and transparent benchmarks.
cross_partisan_appeals.evidence: Housing element compliance frameworks can pair unit pipelines with capital planning and phased implementation.
cross_partisan_appeals.example_dialogue: "Accountability means not just approving units on paper, but matching growth with roads, schools, and utilities."

cross_partisan_appeals.framing: More legal housing options reduce bidding wars and displacement pressure.
cross_partisan_appeals.evidence: Regions that add supply across price points typically see slower rent growth than constrained markets.
cross_partisan_appeals.example_dialogue: "When every neighborhood adds homes, fewer families get pushed farther away each year."

cross_partisan_appeals.framing: Follow state law consistently to avoid costly litigation and uncertainty.
cross_partisan_appeals.evidence: Noncompliance with housing mandates can trigger legal risk, project delays, and loss of local control.
cross_partisan_appeals.example_dialogue: "Even if you dislike Sacramento mandates, predictable local compliance protects taxpayers and local decision-making."

common_ground: Teachers, nurses, and working families should be able to live in Orange County.
Cities should keep commitments they adopt in official plans.
Growth should be transparent, lawful, and infrastructure-aware.
conversation_traps_to_avoid: Avoid framing all homeowner concerns as selfishness.
Avoid promising that RHNA compliance alone solves affordability.
Avoid city-vs-city blame that undermines countywide solutions.
when_to_acknowledge_uncertainty: Exact pace of project delivery after entitlement approvals.
Interest-rate effects on actual construction starts.
School enrollment and traffic impacts by micro-area.
honest_limits: We cannot compel private builders to start projects on our timeline.
We cannot replace city planning departments or elected councils.
We cannot guarantee deed-restricted affordability outcomes without funding partnerships.
